question: According to the lecture, what visual gadget is used in the Super Mario Brothers reduction to handle the case where variable wires cross each other, and what two directions of traversal does it permit?
answer: The crossover gadget is used to handle crossing wires in the Super Mario Brothers reduction. It is described as a unidirectional crossover gadget that permits traversal either from left to right or from bottom to top, but does not allow leakage between the two paths, such as going from bottom to right or from left to bottom.

ground_truth_spans.description: Introduction of the crossover gadget problem when wires cross
Description of the unidirectional crossover gadget and its valid traversal directions
Frame caption showing the lecture diagram of the crossover gadget in context of the Mario level

question: The lecture begins by listing the chain of reductions that will be covered. According to both the spoken content and the visible board diagrams, what is the first problem in the reduction chain and what problem does it reduce to first?
answer: The first problem in the reduction chain is 3SAT, which is the granddaddy of all NP-complete problems. According to the lecture, 3SAT reduces to Super Mario Brothers first, as the instructor states they will reduce from 3SAT to Super Mario Brothers and also to 3-Dimensional Matching, building a chain of NP-hardness results.

ground_truth_spans.description: Whiteboard caption listing the reduction chain starting with Super Mario Bros. and 3SAT
Spoken description of the full reduction chain starting from 3SAT to Super Mario Brothers

question: In the Super Mario Brothers NP-hardness proof, what object is inside the question mark block in the clause gadget, and why must it be activated before Mario can traverse the clause?
answer: Inside the question mark block in the clause gadget is an invincibility star. The star must be activated because the clause contains flaming bars of death that Mario cannot pass through without the star's invincibility. If none of the three literals in the clause are set to true, no star will be available and Mario will die trying to traverse, making it impossible to finish the level unless the clause is satisfied.

ground_truth_spans.description: Description of the invincibility star inside the question mark block in the clause gadget
Frame caption showing lecture diagram of the clause gadget with question mark blocks and star mechanics

question: According to the lecture's definition written on the board and the spoken explanation, what two conditions must both be satisfied for a problem X to be classified as NP-complete?
answer: For a problem X to be NP-complete, two conditions must both be satisfied: first, X must be in NP, meaning it has polynomial-size certificates and polynomial-time verifiers for YES inputs, or equivalently a nondeterministic polynomial-time algorithm. Second, X must be NP-hard, meaning every problem in NP reduces to X via a polynomial-time algorithm that converts inputs of one problem to equivalent inputs of another.

ground_truth_spans.description: Spoken definition of NP-complete requiring X in NP and X NP-hard
Board caption showing written definition: X is NP-complete if X in NP and X is NP-hard, and X is NP-hard if every problem in NP reduces to X

question: In the 3-Dimensional Matching variable gadget shown on the board during the lecture, how many ways can the gadget be solved locally, and what do the two colorings represent in terms of the variable assignment?
answer: The variable gadget in the 3-Dimensional Matching reduction can be solved locally in exactly two ways, corresponding to a wheel structure with colored triangles. The two colorings, represented as blue triangles and red triangles, correspond to the two possible truth assignments for the variable: one coloring represents the variable being set to true and the other represents it being set to false. You cannot mix and match the colors because that would cause either overlap or uncovered points.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation of exactly two ways to solve the variable gadget locally using red and blue triangles
Frame caption showing the variable gadget diagram with wheel structure and colored triangles

question: Based on both the board diagrams and the spoken lecture, what is the specific base B chosen for the number representation in the reduction from 3-Dimensional Matching to Subset Sum, and why was this particular value chosen?
answer: The base B is chosen to be 1 plus the maximum over all variables of n_xi, where n_xi is the number of occurrences of variable x_i in the formula in either true or false form. This value is chosen because it is large enough to guarantee that when summing numbers in this base, there will never be a carry: since the maximum number of ones that can collide in any digit position is at most the maximum occurrence count, setting the base one larger than this maximum ensures no carries occur, keeping digit values as clean zeros or ones.

ground_truth_spans.description: Spoken explanation of base B = 1 + max of n_xi and why this value was chosen
Board caption showing the formula: base b = 1 + max n_xi with the triple encoding

question: In the rectangle packing reduction from Partition shown on the board, what are the dimensions of the individual rectangles and the target rectangle T, and why must the rectangles be placed horizontally rather than vertically?
answer: Each integer a_i from the partition instance is converted into a 1-by-3a_i rectangle, and the target rectangle T has dimensions 2 by 3 times the sum of all A's divided by 2. The rectangles must be placed horizontally rather than vertically because each rectangle is at least 3 units long and the target rectangle is only 2 units tall, so a rectangle cannot be rotated to fit vertically. This forces all rectangles into one of two horizontal rows, and the total lengths in each row must equal half the sum, which is exactly the partition constraint.

ground_truth_spans.description: Spoken explanation of 1-by-3a_i rectangles and the 2-by-3-sum target rectangle T
Board caption showing the rectangle packing diagram with dimensions and the partition reduction setup

question: According to the frame captions describing the jigsaw puzzle diagram shown on the board, what structural property do the jigsaw pieces have in terms of their unit size and side types?
answer: According to the frame captions describing the jigsaw puzzle diagram, each jigsaw piece is built on a unit square. On each side of the unit square, the side can be one of three types: flat, a tab (protruding outward), or a pocket (indented inward). Each tab and pocket also has a specific shape associated with it, and tabs and pockets of different shapes do not fit together, enabling the pieces to be designed so that only specific pieces can match with each other.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption describing the jigsaw puzzle piece structure with unit squares, flat sides, tabs, and pockets

question: The lecture distinguishes between two different definitions of NP that are equivalent. What are these two equivalent ways of defining NP, and what is the connection that makes them equivalent?
answer: The first definition is that NP is the set of decision problems solvable in nondeterministic polynomial time, where a nondeterministic machine can guess among polynomially many options in constant time, always guessing correctly toward a YES answer if one exists. The second equivalent definition is that NP is the set of decision problems with polynomial-size certificates and polynomial-time verifiers for YES inputs. The connection is that a nondeterministic algorithm can be rewritten with all guessing at the front, and the collection of guesses forms the certificate; a deterministic polynomial-time verifier then checks whether those guesses actually prove the answer is YES.

ground_truth_spans.description: Definition of NP as nondeterministic polynomial time with lucky guessing toward YES answers
Equivalent definition of NP as decision problems with polynomial-size certificates and polynomial-time verifiers, and explanation of the connection

question: The lecture explains why weak NP-hardness makes it impossible to directly reduce Partition to Jigsaw Puzzles. What is the specific reason this reduction fails, and what stronger form of NP-hardness is needed to make the reduction to Jigsaw Puzzles work?
answer: The reduction from Partition to Jigsaw Puzzles fails because the numbers in the Partition instance produced by the reduction from Subset Sum are exponentially large. When converting an integer a_i into that many tiles, the number of jigsaw pieces becomes exponential in N, which is not allowed since a valid reduction must be polynomial in size. Strong NP-hardness is needed instead, because in a strongly NP-hard problem the values of the numbers are bounded by a polynomial in the input size, so representing each integer in unary with that many tiles produces only polynomially many pieces, making the reduction valid.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation of why partition's exponentially large numbers make the jigsaw puzzle reduction invalid
Explanation that strong NP-hardness is needed, requiring polynomial-valued numbers for the unary tile representation to work

question: The lecture explains that proving 3SAT NP-complete for the first time was done in the 1970s and required a different approach than what students need to do today. Who originally proved 3SAT NP-complete, what did that original proof require, and what shortcut is available to students now?
answer: 3SAT was originally proved NP-complete in the 1970s by Cook. That original proof required starting from any arbitrary problem in NP and showing that it could be reduced to 3SAT, which was tedious because there are many problems in the world. The shortcut available to students today is that since 3SAT is already known to be NP-complete, one only needs to reduce from a single known NP-complete problem such as 3SAT to the new problem being proved hard, rather than reducing from every problem in NP.

ground_truth_spans.description: Cook proved 3SAT NP-complete in the 70s by reducing every NP problem to 3SAT
Students now only need to reduce from one known NP-complete problem to the new problem, rather than from all NP problems

question: What generalization of the original Super Mario Brothers game does the lecture use for the NP-hardness proof, and why is this generalization necessary?
answer: The lecture generalizes Super Mario Brothers to an arbitrary screen size of n-by-n, rather than the original fixed 320-by-240 screen. This generalization is necessary because if the screen size is fixed and constant, you can use dynamic programming to find the optimal solution in polynomial time. With a fixed screen size the problem is tractable, but with an arbitrary screen size that grows with the input, the problem becomes NP-hard. There is also no time limit constraint in the generalized version, or the time limit must be generalized to an arbitrary polynomial value rather than a fixed 300 seconds.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that the generalization to arbitrary n-by-n screen size is needed because a fixed screen size allows dynamic programming in polynomial time

question: What is the difference between weakly NP-hard and strongly NP-hard, and which of the two categories does Subset Sum fall into according to the lecture?
answer: A problem is weakly NP-hard if it is NP-hard but becomes solvable in polynomial time when the numeric values in the input are bounded by a polynomial in the input size; in this case only pseudo-polynomial time algorithms exist. A problem is strongly NP-hard if it remains NP-hard even when the values of all numbers are bounded by a polynomial in n. Subset Sum falls into the weakly NP-hard category because when the numbers have polynomial value, a pseudo-polynomial time dynamic programming algorithm exists, running in time polynomial in the numeric values of the numbers, specifically resembling the knapsack algorithm.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation distinguishing weakly NP-hard from strongly NP-hard, and Subset Sum being weakly NP-hard with a pseudo-polynomial algorithm
